content.full_text:
Fern Por-rrrcAI- Pnecrrcps CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suire 620 r Sacramenro. CA 95g14_2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
October 20,2009
Robert Zweben
Albany City Attomey
1730 Solano Avenue
Albany, CA94706
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-09-219
Dear Mr. Zweben:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Albany Mayor Marge
Atkinson regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform i"t 1it " 
.:a"l;|.,
Please note that our advice is based solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no
opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict
of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090.
In addition, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(bX8XA).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective
actions. Also note our advice is based solely on the facts presented in you, i"qu"st; the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (t, ," Oglesby (lg7S) I
FPPC Ops.7l.)
QUESTIONS
1. Can city council decisions to apply for funding from another agency for particular
projects be "segmented," so that the mayor may recuse herself from votin! o, ih, pioject located
within 500 feet of her home and then vote on the other projects?
2. If three out of the five members of the city council, including the mayor,
have a disqualifying conflict of interest in.decisions to apply for projecifunding, may the mayor
vote, notwithstanding her conflict, under the "legally required parricipation,, ex-ception?
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of ritle z of ttrJcatitbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


Our File No. 4-09-219
Page2
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. Decisions to apply for funding for individual projects are inextricably
interrelated. Because the amount of money available for funding projects is limited and is
insufficient to fully fund all projects, a decision to reject or underfund one project could free up
money for the project in which the mayor has a conflict of interest.
2' One of the councilmembers with a disqualifying conflict of interest may vote under
th-e "legally required participation" exception of Rigulaiio" tszos. However, a random means
of selection must be used to select this councilmember. see the discussion under.,Analysis,,
below for more detail.
F'ACTS
The City of Albany intends to apply for funding from the East Bay Regional park District
(the "District") for various projects that would expand-and improve tt " .ity," park and recreation
facilities. The funding is available through the District's Measure WW allocation of funds to
cities-based on population. The City of .{tbuny has been allocated $771,363 and has up to the
yeat 2018 to use the funds. To obtain this funding, cities submit applications that include a
specific dollar amount requested, for each individual project.
At the direction of the city council, the city's Parks and Recreation Commission (the
"Commission"), with input from the Waterfront Committee, has studied potential projects,
compiled a list of thirteen projects and made recommendations to the city council. City staff has
also made recommendatio_ns regarding the thirteen projects. The city council is now selecting
fro-m that list the projects for which funding will be^sought and deciiing the dollar amount that
will be requested for each project.
one vote has.already taken place for funding of a project to acquire land for ,.pierce
Street Park"' Councilmembers Thomsen and Javandel recused themseives from discussions and
voting on this project because they believed they had a disqualifying conflict of inrerest. They
have indicated that they will also recuse themseives from future votes on any project funding
because they believe those votes could affect the project in which they have a conflict of interest.
Their belief is.based on the fact that the city "our"il 
-has 
the power to revisit a decision regarding
a particular project, including the pierce Street park project.
Mayor Atkinson lives within 500 feet of one of the other projects (..ohlone Greenway,,)
that has not yet been voted on by the city council.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ..perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section g1001(b).) Section g7100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or orherwise r-rsing his or her


Our File No. 4-09-219
Page 3
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Your request for advice assumes that Mayor Atkinson has a conflict of interest with
respect to any city council decision regarding funding for the Ohlone Greenway project. you
inquire only as to (l) whether she may vote on projects other than the ohlone i;.".n*oy project
on the basis that a decision to fund the Ohlone Grein*oy project may be .,segmented,, from
decisions to fund other projects and (2) if she is prohibitld rro* voting on ttE f-iecrs because
the decisions cannot be_segm-ented, may she nevertheless vote on all projects, ir.il;;;,h";;;
in which she has a conflict of interest, under the "legally required participation; exception.
Segmentation
Segmentation is generally applied to a large and complex decision which, under certain
lircumllances, may be divided into separate decisions, thus enabling an official who has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in one component of the decision tJ participate as to other
components in which the official has no financial interest. (Regulation tAZOg; Merkutoff Advice
Letter, No. I-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) Under your facts, it appears that
decisions to apply for funding for each individual project are already made separately. However,
the decisions may be so inextricably interrelated, tiruiur" of "segmentation,, analysis as a model
is appropriate.
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financiai inrerest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official,s
participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified pu.blic official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was'dislualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated,, when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nrltify, or uti.. tfr"
result of another decision.


Our File No. 4-09-219
Page 4
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions
that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which Mayor Atkinson has a disqualifying
conflict of interest. Under the guidelines of this regulation, the decision regarding the project"
near the mayor's home and decisions regarding the other projects would hive to be segmented
and the decision affecting the mayor's economic interest must be voted on before the other
decisions may be voted on.
UnderRegulation 18709(b), if theresolutionof onedecisionwilleffectively alterthe
result of another decision, the decisions 1e inextricably related. (See Thomsen Advice Letter,
No. 4-09-1 59; Yang Advice Letter, No. I-06_19g; Stoie Advice Letter, No. 4_06_0 07; Barker
Advice Letter, No. A-05-164; Hull Advice Letter, No. 4-04-052.) Also, in the past, the
Commission has advised that if the resolution of one decision wili in any way affictthe decision
from which a public official is disqualified, the decisions may not be segment ed. (Barker
Advice Letter, No. 4-03-022; McLaugtin Advice Letter, No. 4-02-132; Ennisaavice Letter, No.
A-94-2O3.)
You have indicated that funds allocated by the District for the city's projects are limited
to $771,363 and that the city council is considering only those projects selected"by the
Commission and staff. It is our undersranding thai$ZZiJA iJiniufficient to fully fund all the
projects' Therefore, decisions to apply for funding in a certain dollar amount for a particular
project will necessarily limit the dollar amount available for other projects. By voting on a
project and either rejecting the project, or setting the dollar amount very low, the city council
would be making more funds available f9r th9 other projects. Also, it is possiUte for the city
council to reconsider prior funding decisions if, afteivoiing on other projech, funds remain
available in the allocation pool. where there is no mechunlr* to ensure that a decision regarding
the ohlone Greenway project is final and cannot be reopened, votes on other prolects could free
up funds for reconsideration of the ohlone Greenway pioject. Under these ciicumstances,
decisions on other projects could both a,lter the resuit of a prior decision regarding the ohlone
Greenway project and affect that prior decision. we conclude that decision"s to apply for District
Il1": tyot be broken down into separate decisions because they are inextricably inrerrelated
oecrslons.
Legally Required Participation (,,Rule of Necessity,,)
Section 87101 provides a mechanism through which a decisionmaking body
can avoid paralysis when it is impossible to assemble a quorum of member, ,iho *"
not disqualified from participating in a decision by Sectiin 87100, rhe Act,s general
rule governing conflicts of interest. Section g7lOi provides as follows:
"Section 87100 does not prevent any public official from making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision to the exient his
participation is legally required for the action or decision to be made. The fact
that an official's vote is needed to break a tie does not make his participation
legally required for puqposes of this section.,,


Our File No. .{-09-219
Page 5
Regulation 18708 elaborates the process by which section 87101 is given effect. In brief,
when a decisionmaking body cannot muster a quorum of members free frori any conflict of
interest, one or more of the disqualified officiati - iust the number to make up a bare quorum -
are selected to take part in the decision at issue, ar,d i., any ancillary decisions where the conflict
persists. (See, generally, Battersby Advice Letter, No. t-dz-t+ I; CroninAdvice Letter,
No. 4-97-579: Pilot Advice Letrer, No. 4"97_265.)
Your letter assumes that there are three councilmembers (out of five) who have conflicts
of interest regarding decisions to apply for project funding. Hence, a quorum of the city council
can only be assembled by means of the "legally required farticipation'i rule of Section g7101 and
Regulation 18708, as described in the Battersiy Ad-vice Letter, supra.
With respect to the method to be employed for selecting which disqualified
councilmember is "legally required" to participate in a decision, we have advised that a random
means of selection mustbe used to select only the number of officials needed. (TtffanyAdvice
Letter, No' I-08-007.) Also, all disqualified councilmembers must participate in the selection
process. (See Steele Advice Letter, No. 4-05-071; Battersby Advice Letter, supra.)
Accordingly, Mayor Atkinson may not be singled out as the councilmember who is legally
required to participate in the decisions.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me
at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
"-"/ru^rt*
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJjgl
